title: Weather Station Data Integration & Altitude Compensation
app: Splunk Edge Hub (optional pressure sensor), MQTT integration
spl:
| mstats avg(pressure) as edge_press by host, _time span=1h
| join max=1 host [| mstats avg(external_pressure) as ext_press by host, _time span=1h]
| eval altitude_diff = (44330 * (1.0 - ((edge_press / ext_press)^(1/5.255))))
| where altitude_diff != 0
| eval altitude_compensated_reading = edge_press - (altitude_diff * 0.0001198)
description: Provides pressure-altitude data for facility environmental baselines and corrects sensor readings for elevation changes.
value: Provides pressure-altitude data for facility environmental baselines and corrects sensor readings for elevation changes.
grandmaExplanation: We give you pressure-altitude data for facility environmental baselines and corrects sensor readings for elevation changes — so faults, tampering, and safety issues are spotted right away.
implementation: Deploy Edge Hub with optional pressure sensor at facility location. Subscribe to external MQTT weather station (Advanced Settings → MQTT Subscriptions) publishing atmospheric pressure. Use barometric formula to compute altitude or detect pressure sensor drift. Store readings in edge-hub-data metric index. Pressure range 300-1100 hPa covers sea-level to 3,000m elevation. Use local SQLite backlog for real-time compensation without cloud latency.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Edge Hub (optional pressure sensor), MQTT integration.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=edge-hub-data` metric_name=pressure, `index=edge-hub-logs` sourcetype=splunk_edge_hub_log external_weather_device.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Deploy Edge Hub with optional pressure sensor at facility location. Subscribe to external MQTT weather station (Advanced Settings → MQTT Subscriptions) publishing atmospheric pressure. Use barometric formula to compute altitude or detect pressure sensor drift. Store readings in edge-hub-data metric index. Pressure range 300-1100 hPa covers sea-level to 3,000m elevation. Use local SQLite backlog for real-time compensation without cloud latency.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| mstats avg(pressure) as edge_press by host, _time span=1h
| join max=1 host [| mstats avg(external_pressure) as ext_press by host, _time span=1h]
| eval altitude_diff = (44330 * (1.0 - ((edge_press / ext_press)^(1/5.255))))
| where altitude_diff != 0
| eval altitude_compensated_reading = edge_press - (altitude_diff * 0.0001198)
```

Understanding this SPL

**Weather Station Data Integration & Altitude Compensation** — Provides pressure-altitude data for facility environmental baselines and corrects sensor readings for elevation changes.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=edge-hub-data` metric_name=pressure, `index=edge-hub-logs` sourcetype=splunk_edge_hub_log external_weather_device. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Edge Hub (optional pressure sensor), MQTT integration. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `mstats` to query metrics indexes (pre-aggregated metric data).
• Joins to a subsearch with `join` — set `max=` to match cardinality and avoid silent truncation.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **altitude_diff** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where altitude_diff != 0` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **altitude_compensated_reading** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Altitude vs time, pressure correction factor trend, weather correlation chart.
visualization: Altitude vs time, pressure correction factor trend, weather correlation chart.
knownFalsePositives: Planned maintenance, backups, or batch jobs can drive metrics outside normal bands — correlate with change management windows.
